***G.I. Joe: Retaliation*** is a 2013 American [military science fiction](/wiki/Military_science_fiction "Military science fiction") [action film](/wiki/Action_film "Action film") based on the *[G.I. Joe](/wiki/G.I._Joe "G.I. Joe")* toy line. It is the second installment in the [*G.I. Joe* film series](/wiki/G.I._Joe_(film_series) "G.I. Joe (film series)") and the stand-alone sequel to *[G.I. Joe: The Rise of Cobra](/wiki/G.I._Joe:_The_Rise_of_Cobra "G.I. Joe: The Rise of Cobra")* (2009). Directed by [Jon M. Chu](/wiki/Jon_M._Chu "Jon M. Chu") and written by [Rhett Reese](/wiki/Rhett_Reese "Rhett Reese") and [Paul Wernick](/wiki/Paul_Wernick "Paul Wernick"), the film features an [ensemble cast](/wiki/Ensemble_cast "Ensemble cast") starring [D. J. Cotrona](/wiki/D._J._Cotrona "D. J. Cotrona"), [Lee Byung-hun](/wiki/Lee_Byung-hun "Lee Byung-hun"), [Adrianne Palicki](/wiki/Adrianne_Palicki "Adrianne Palicki"), [Ray Park](/wiki/Ray_Park "Ray Park"), [Jonathan Pryce](/wiki/Jonathan_Pryce "Jonathan Pryce"), [Ray Stevenson](/wiki/Ray_Stevenson "Ray Stevenson"), [Channing Tatum](/wiki/Channing_Tatum "Channing Tatum"), [Bruce Willis](/wiki/Bruce_Willis "Bruce Willis"), and [Dwayne Johnson](/wiki/Dwayne_Johnson "Dwayne Johnson").[[7]](#cite_note-7) The G.I. Joes fight their enemy Cobra again and also face threats from within the US government.[[8]](#cite_note-metacritic-8)

Originally slated for release in June 2012, the film was delayed to convert to [3D](/wiki/3D_film "3D film") and boost interest in international markets. *G.I. Joe: Retaliation* was released in the United States on March 28, 2013, by [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures"). The film, like its predecessor, received generally negative reviews from critics, although it was considered an improvement from its predecessor and grossed $375.7 million worldwide against a budget of $130–155 million.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

Special Forces team commander [Duke](/wiki/Duke_(G.I._Joe) "Duke (G.I. Joe)") leads the [G.I. Joe Team](/wiki/G.I._Joe_Team "G.I. Joe Team") to the [Korean Demilitarized Zone](/wiki/Korean_Demilitarized_Zone "Korean Demilitarized Zone") to rescue a North Korean defector. They are then assigned another mission to steal nuclear warheads from [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") after the death of [its President](/wiki/President_of_Pakistan "President of Pakistan") during a civil war. Deeming them as traitors for causing an international incident, [Zartan](/wiki/Zartan "Zartan"), still impersonating the [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), calls an airstrike that kills Duke and many Joes. Heavy machine gunner [Roadblock](/wiki/Roadblock_(G.I._Joe) "Roadblock (G.I. Joe)"), sniper [Flint](/wiki/Flint_(G.I._Joe) "Flint (G.I. Joe)"), and covert operator [Lady Jaye](/wiki/Lady_Jaye "Lady Jaye") survive the attack by diving into a well, and they return to the United States to contact [General Joseph Colton](/wiki/General_Joseph_Colton "General Joseph Colton"), who provides them with weapons.

Meanwhile, [Storm Shadow](/wiki/Storm_Shadow_(G.I._Joe) "Storm Shadow (G.I. Joe)"), who survived the Arctic base's destruction,[[a]](#cite_note-9) and demolitions expert [Firefly](/wiki/Firefly_(G.I._Joe) "Firefly (G.I. Joe)") rescue [Cobra Commander](/wiki/Cobra_Commander "Cobra Commander") from an underground maximum-security prison in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), leaving [Destro](/wiki/Destro "Destro") behind. Storm Shadow is injured and retreats to a temple in the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") to recover. The Blind Master, leader of the [Arashikage](/wiki/Arashikage "Arashikage") Clan, sends [Snake Eyes](/wiki/Snake_Eyes_(G.I._Joe) "Snake Eyes (G.I. Joe)") and his apprentice [Jinx](/wiki/Jinx_(G.I._Joe) "Jinx (G.I. Joe)"), to capture Storm Shadow, allowing him to answer for the murder of his uncle, the [Hard Master](/wiki/Hard_Master "Hard Master").

After Zartan announces that Cobra will become the premier U.S. special forces team, replacing G.I. Joe, Lady Jaye deduces that someone is impersonating the President by noting changes in his speech patterns. At a presidential fundraiser, she steals a sample of the President's DNA, confirming that he is Zartan. They escape after a confrontation with Firefly and [Zandar](/wiki/Zandar "Zandar"), the head of the [U.S. Secret Service](/wiki/U.S._Secret_Service "U.S. Secret Service")'s Presidential Detail and a member of Cobra.

Snake Eyes and Jinx locate and capture Storm Shadow after a battle with some ninjas loyal to Storm Shadow and take him back to [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), where Storm Shadow proclaims that he did not kill the Hard Master and proves it by fighting Snake Eyes with the weapon that killed the Hard Master, which breaks as it is not Arashikage steel. The Blind Master deduces that Zartan was the one that murdered the Hard Master and framed Storm Shadow for it, who joined Cobra to bring it down from the inside. Exonerated, Storm Shadow accompanies Snake Eyes and Jinx, his cousin, as they join the Joes' efforts to stop Cobra and avenge the Hard Master.

Zartan invites the world leaders to a summit at [Fort Sumter](/wiki/Fort_Sumter "Fort Sumter"), where he launches the entire US [nuclear arsenal](/wiki/Nuclear_arsenal "Nuclear arsenal"), forcing the other nuclear powers to do the same. All the world leaders activate the self-destruct for their nuclear arsenals, removing the justification for the retaliation strikes and goads to save the Earth. Zartan proclaims this a nuclear free world and unveils Project Zeus, orbital [kinetic bombardment](/wiki/Kinetic_bombardment "Kinetic bombardment") weapons he designed for Cobra. To prove his superiority, he destroys Central [London](/wiki/London "London") and activates the remaining weapons, offering to disarm them if the leaders submit to Cobra. Storm Shadow betrays Cobra Commander and starts a fight, acknowledging Cobra's deception to the world leaders. Colton kills Zandar and rescues the real President with Lady Jaye, while Storm Shadow kills Zartan. While Snake Eyes, Jinx, and Flint fight Cobra's soldiers, Cobra Commander instructs Firefly to protect the launch device, and escapes by helicopter. Roadblock overpowers Firefly and destroys the orbital weapons. Storm Shadow leaves after avenging his uncle.

At the White House ceremony, the real President addresses the nation and commemorates the Joes, who were awarded by Colton: Roadblock, Snake Eyes, Jinx, Flint, and Lady Jaye. Colton presents Roadblock with an [M1911 pistol](/wiki/M1911_pistol "M1911 pistol") that belonged to General [George S. Patton](/wiki/George_S._Patton "George S. Patton"), to be used when they find Cobra Commander. Roadblock proudly raises the weapon and fires a single shot in honor of his fallen comrades, vowing to avenge them.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development")]

After the financially successful release of *[The Rise of Cobra](/wiki/G.I._Joe:_The_Rise_of_Cobra "G.I. Joe: The Rise of Cobra")*, Rob Moore, the studio vice chairman of [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures"), stated in 2009 that a sequel would be developed. In January 2011, Rhett Reese and [Paul Wernick](/wiki/Paul_Wernick "Paul Wernick"), the writers of *[Zombieland](/wiki/Zombieland "Zombieland")*, were hired to write the script.[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12) The movie was originally thought to be titled *G.I. Joe: Cobra Strikes*,[[12]](#cite_note-CobraStrikesTitle-13) which was later denied by Reese.[[13]](#cite_note-14) [Stephen Sommers](/wiki/Stephen_Sommers "Stephen Sommers") was originally going to return as director of the sequel, but Paramount Pictures announced in February 2011 that [Jon Chu](/wiki/Jon_Chu "Jon Chu") would direct the sequel.[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16) In July 2011, the sequel's name was revealed to be *G.I. Joe: Retaliation*.[[16]](#cite_note-IGN-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18) Chu would later declare that Paramount wanted a [reboot](/wiki/Reboot_(fiction) "Reboot (fiction)") that also served as a sequel to *The Rise of Cobra* since "a lot of people saw the first movie so we don't want to alienate that and redo the whole thing."[[18]](#cite_note-superherohype-19)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Filming")]

[Principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") began in August 2011 in [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana").[[16]](#cite_note-IGN-17)[[39]](#cite_note-40)

The battle on the Himalayas was shot in the south vertical assembly building at NASA's [Michoud Assembly Facility](/wiki/Michoud_Assembly_Facility "Michoud Assembly Facility"), that had been fitted with a [green screen](/wiki/Green_screen "Green screen") wall at a very steep angle with a lot of rigging above to swing the stunt people through.[[40]](#cite_note-awn-41)

[Fort Pike](/wiki/Fort_Pike "Fort Pike") in Louisiana stands in for [Fort Sumter](/wiki/Fort_Sumter "Fort Sumter") in [South Carolina](/wiki/South_Carolina "South Carolina") as the site of the climactic summit meeting of the leaders of nuclear-armed countries.

#### Fatality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Fatality")]

On November 22, 2011, a crew member, Mike Huber,[[41]](#cite_note-42) died in an accident at a New Orleans warehouse used as a soundstage for the film. Crew members were changing out a set,[[42]](#cite_note-43) and the [scissor lift](/wiki/Scissor_lift "Scissor lift") Huber was operating fell on him.[[43]](#cite_note-44) [OSHA](/wiki/OSHA "OSHA") assessed Paramount for a $21,000 penalty, some five years later the penalty was still being contested.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

### Visual effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Visual effects")]

*Retaliation* had 700 visual effects shots,[[45]](#cite_note-dd-46) which were mostly handled by three effects companies.[[40]](#cite_note-awn-41) Visual effects supervisor Zachary Kinnery declared that while the visuals aimed for the "big and bold" typical of the franchise, *Retaliation* would be the first to attempt "a bit more of that gritty realism."[[46]](#cite_note-47) The major part of the effects was given to [Digital Domain](/wiki/Digital_Domain "Digital Domain"), which for 227 effects created digital vehicles and aircraft that had to "look fantastic but which are also plausible", given they had to match practical models, the Zeus satellite and a sequence where Zartan shows his nanomite-related disguise to the president—done with the same head replacement software developed for *[The Curious Case of Benjamin Button](/wiki/The_Curious_Case_of_Benjamin_Button_(film) "The Curious Case of Benjamin Button (film)")* and *[Tron: Legacy](/wiki/Tron:_Legacy "Tron: Legacy")*. [Industrial Light & Magic](/wiki/Industrial_Light_%26_Magic "Industrial Light & Magic") made the London destruction, a digital White House, and the mountain sword fight, which had computer-generated backgrounds and digital augmentation of the stunt people's performance. [Method Studios](/wiki/Method_Studios "Method Studios") was responsible for the desert attack, Firefly's explosive bugs, and malfunction on underground prison.[[40]](#cite_note-awn-41)[[45]](#cite_note-dd-46)[[47]](#cite_note-48) Saints LA handled minor effects such as [compositing](/wiki/Compositing "Compositing") and news graphics.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

A soundtrack of the score was released in April 2013.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Release")]

### Theatrical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Theatrical")]

Previously slated for release on August 10, 2012[[12]](#cite_note-CobraStrikesTitle-13) and June 29, 2012,[[50]](#cite_note-51) Paramount announced in May 2012 that they were delaying the film's release until March 29, 2013 (but was later moved up to March 28, 2013), in order to convert the movie to 3D and boost interest in international markets.[[51]](#cite_note-Mar13-52)[[52]](#cite_note-53)

The delay "gobsmacked" the film industry due to Paramount's implementation of a substantial advertising campaign beginning with a [Super Bowl commercial](/wiki/Super_Bowl_commercial "Super Bowl commercial"), with the "warehouses full of" toys were waiting for the film's launch, and because it was one of only three Paramount-produced films scheduled for summer 2012 (along with *[The Dictator](/wiki/The_Dictator_(2012_film) "The Dictator (2012 film)")* and *[Katy Perry: Part of Me](/wiki/Katy_Perry:_Part_of_Me "Katy Perry: Part of Me")*). The studio also wanted to avoid competing with Tatum's *[Magic Mike](/wiki/Magic_Mike "Magic Mike")*, also scheduled for June 29.[[53]](#cite_note-dh20120529-54)

### Ban in Pakistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ban in Pakistan")]

The film was banned by the [Central Board of Film Censors](/wiki/Central_Board_of_Film_Censors "Central Board of Film Censors") of [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") due to initial scenes at the beginning of the movie which depict the country negatively, according to film censor board officials. A [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi")-based cinema posted on its Facebook page that the film would not be screened due to restrictions by the censor board. The censorship was due to the film's depiction of Pakistan as an unstable state and the fictional portrayal of a "foreign invasion of Pakistan's nuclear installations", which caught the ire of film censor authorities. Consequently, restrictions were imposed on screening the movie countrywide.[[54]](#cite_note-55)[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58) According to an official at the censor board, the film portrayed Pakistan negatively not only on the issue of the [war on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror") but also on the international standing of the country, "There is a scene which shows the assassination of the Pakistani president and the imposition of martial law, which is not a fair representation of the country."[[58]](#cite_note-et-59) Another cinema official said, "There were obviously several objectionable things which would never have passed the censors, but these things are also relevant to the content of the film."[[58]](#cite_note-et-59)

### Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Marketing")]

A toyline for the film was confirmed by [Hasbro](/wiki/Hasbro "Hasbro") in February 2012.[[59]](#cite_note-60) Despite the movie's release being moved from June 2012 to March 2013, the initial assortments of figures, vehicles, and role-play items were shipped to retailers, and appeared on store shelves in May 2012. A *Variety* article was published stating that the already released figures had been pulled from the shelves and recalled by Hasbro,[[60]](#cite_note-61) although the company's official statement indicated that existing product would be sold through. New product shipments were halted by Hasbro, but existing *Retaliation* figures were available in Target, Wal-Mart, and Toys R Us as late as December 2012.[[61]](#cite_note-62) The toyline was re-released in the United States in February 2013.[[62]](#cite_note-63) A four-part limited series comic book titled *G.I. Joe: Retaliation Movie Prequel* was published by [IDW Publishing](/wiki/IDW_Publishing "IDW Publishing") from February 2012 to April 2012. Written by John Barber, it acts as a prequel to the movie.

### Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Home media")]

*G.I. Joe: Retaliation* was released on [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD"), [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray"), and [Blu-ray 3D](/wiki/Blu-ray_3D "Blu-ray 3D") on July 30, 2013, by [Paramount Home Entertainment](/wiki/Paramount_Home_Entertainment "Paramount Home Entertainment").[[63]](#cite_note-64) A Blu-ray "Extended Action Cut" added 12 minutes of footage and uncensored violence was also available, with the United States version being a [Best Buy](/wiki/Best_Buy "Best Buy") exclusive.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

The film topped No. 1 on both the Blu-ray and DVD sales charts with at least 54% of both Blu-ray and DVD units sold.[[65]](#cite_note-66) The film also topped weekend rentals too.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

It was later released on [Ultra HD Blu-ray](/wiki/Ultra_HD_Blu-ray "Ultra HD Blu-ray") on July 20, 2021, along with the first movie, to coincide with the theatrical release of *[Snake Eyes](/wiki/Snake_Eyes_(2021_film) "Snake Eyes (2021 film)")*.

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Box office")]

*G.I. Joe: Retaliation* grossed $122.5 million in North America, $253.2 million internationally, and $375.7 million worldwide total,[[5]](#cite_note-mojo-5) to a budget of $130–$155 million.[[1]](#cite_note-Forbes-1)[[6]](#cite_note-SnakeEyesOctober2020-6)

In North America, the film grossed $10.5 million on its opening day, debuting at the top of the box office.[[67]](#cite_note-68) The film retained the No. 1 spot over the three-day weekend and grossed $40.5 million; however, the film's opening weekend fell 14.2% against *The Rise of Cobra*'s $54.7 million debut.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Critical response")]

On [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes") it has an approval rating of 29% based on 180 reviews, with an average rating of 4.5/10. The website's critical consensus reads, "Though arguably superior to its predecessor, *G.I. Joe: Retaliation* is overwhelmed by its nonstop action and too nonsensical and vapid to leave a lasting impression."[[69]](#cite_note-tomatoes-70) On [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), the film has a [weighted average](/wiki/Weighted_arithmetic_mean "Weighted arithmetic mean") score of 41 out of 100, based on 31 critics, indicating "mixed or average" reviews.[[8]](#cite_note-metacritic-8) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "A−" on an A+ to F scale.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

Alan Scherstuhl of *[The Village Voice](/wiki/The_Village_Voice "The Village Voice")* wrote in a positive review that "this [movie] pushes right past *competent* into *mostly legitimately enjoyable*" but added that "the movie is still dumb as catbutt. It's an honest and accomplished dumbness, however, where the stupidest stuff seems to be there because the movie would be less fun without it."[[71]](#cite_note-72) *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")*'s Todd McCarthy was critical about the film's use of 3D and accurate reflection of the franchise's comic book and cartoon origins, but predicted it would still earn better than its predecessor, *[G.I. Joe: The Rise of Cobra](/wiki/G.I._Joe:_The_Rise_of_Cobra "G.I. Joe: The Rise of Cobra")*.[[72]](#cite_note-McCarthy-73) Owen Gleiberman of *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* gave the film a grade of "B−", calling it "well-executed technocratic action fluff" and commented: "In its dehumanized and trivial way, it's a triumph of razor-sharp, hyper-violent style over formulaic substance ... Hollywood has now evolved to the point that it can deliver these kinds of thrills with maximum brute force and keep the impact so light that the result can *still* be regarded as a 'harmless' diversion for 14-year-olds."[[73]](#cite_note-Owen-74) Glen Heath Jr. of *[Slant Magazine](/wiki/Slant_Magazine "Slant Magazine")* gave it two out of four stars, criticizing the film's "cut-happy style" and plot, but lauding the action sequences and Chu's direction as "poetry in high-speed motion."[[74]](#cite_note-Heath-75)

In a negative review, Betsey Sharkey of the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* panned the "overwhelmingly complicated, globe-hopping, enemies within, enemies without story line" and 3D but noted that "the humor, when it works, offers 'Retaliation' some redemption." She ended with: "It's convoluted. Frankly no one should have to think that hard to keep up with the Joes."[[75]](#cite_note-Sharkey-76) Another negative review came from *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* magazine's Justin Chang, who ridiculed the movie's large-scale destruction of foreign cities, writing: "Audiences who thrilled to the sight of Paris under biochemical attack in *Cobra* will be pleased to watch London endure an even more horrific fate here, although the sequence is tossed off in quick, almost ho-hum fashion, with no time to dwell on anything so exquisitely crass as the spectacle of the Eiffel Tower collapsing." He summarized the movie as "a more straight-faced brand of idiocy than its cheerfully dumb 2009 predecessor."[[76]](#cite_note-Chang-77)

Writing for *[Empire](/wiki/Empire_(film_magazine) "Empire (film magazine)")* magazine, Olly Richards gave the movie 2 stars out of 5 and compared it unfavorably with its predecessor, writing: "The first film you could at least laugh *at*. This takes all its silly ingredients and smushes them down flat. 'Retaliation' over-promises and under-delivers."[[77]](#cite_note-Richards-78) Richard Roeper of *[Chicago Sun-Times](/wiki/Chicago_Sun-Times "Chicago Sun-Times")* gave the movie 1.5 stars out of a possible four, branding it a "ridiculous and overblown debacle" that contained "nothing but well-packaged garbage" and further adding: "To say 'G.I. Joe: Retaliation' is a video game for the big screen is to insult a number of video games that are far more creative, challenging and better-looking."[[78]](#cite_note-Roeper-79)

### Accolades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Accolades")]

At the [2013 Teen Choice Awards](/wiki/2013_Teen_Choice_Awards "2013 Teen Choice Awards"), *G.I. Joe: Retaliation* received four nominations (including [Choice Movie: Action](/wiki/Teen_Choice_Award_for_Choice_Movie_-_Action "Teen Choice Award for Choice Movie - Action")).[[79]](#cite_note-80)[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82) Johnson was nominated for Favorite Male Buttkicker at the [2014 Kids' Choice Awards](/wiki/2014_Kids%27_Choice_Awards "2014 Kids' Choice Awards").[[82]](#cite_note-83) The film was also nominated twice at the [Golden Trailer Awards](/wiki/Golden_Trailer_Awards "Golden Trailer Awards") for Best Action.[[83]](#cite_note-84)[[84]](#cite_note-85)[[85]](#cite_note-86)

## Potential sequel and reboot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=G.I._Joe:_Retaliation&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Potential sequel and reboot")]

In April 2013, reports surfaced that there would be a third *G.I. Joe* film,[[86]](#cite_note-87) and it would likely be in [3D](/wiki/3D_film "3D film").[[87]](#cite_note-88)[[88]](#cite_note-89) The studio announced that Chu would return to direct the third film.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

While at the 2013 [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con"), Chu talked about bringing [Scarlett](/wiki/Scarlett_(G.I._Joe) "Scarlett (G.I. Joe)") back in the next film.[[90]](#cite_note-91) The writers of the second film are also thinking about bringing back the [Baroness](/wiki/Baroness_(G.I._Joe) "Baroness (G.I. Joe)") in the sequel.[[91]](#cite_note-92) Johnson is interested in returning as Roadblock for the sequel,[[92]](#cite_note-93) and Park has talked about a possible return as Snake Eyes and also including his pet wolf Timber.[[93]](#cite_note-94) Producer [Lorenzo di Bonaventura](/wiki/Lorenzo_di_Bonaventura "Lorenzo di Bonaventura") has stated he is open to doing a *G.I. Joe/[Transformers](/wiki/Transformers_(film_series) "Transformers (film series)")* [crossover](/wiki/Crossover_(fiction) "Crossover (fiction)"),[[94]](#cite_note-95) which Chu stated that he would be interested in directing.[[95]](#cite_note-96) Bonaventura told *Beijing News* that he hoped that Johnson and Willis would return, the script is still in the writing stage, and that they are considering adding a third important role.[[96]](#cite_note-97)

By September of the same year, Chu was confirmed to direct the film, along with *[Snow White and the Huntsman](/wiki/Snow_White_and_the_Huntsman "Snow White and the Huntsman")* writer [Evan Daugherty](/wiki/Evan_Daugherty "Evan Daugherty") to pen the film's script.[[97]](#cite_note-98) On December 5, 2013, Daugherty talked about writing the film's script and his feelings about Duke being killed,[[98]](#cite_note-99) but Chu told MTV that Tatum may return as Duke in the sequel.[[99]](#cite_note-100) In an interview with *Collider*, Johnson stated that he believes Chu may not return to direct, due to working on the [live-action *Jem* film](/wiki/Jem_and_the_Holograms_(film) "Jem and the Holograms (film)"), but they may find another director for the film.[[100]](#cite_note-101) In June 2014, di Bonaventura told *Collider* in an interview that they're meeting with new directors and filming may start in early 2015.[[101]](#cite_note-102) The following month, *Variety* reported that [Jonathan Lemkin](/wiki/Jonathan_Lemkin "Jonathan Lemkin") will write the script for the film and will focus on Roadblock with Johnson returning.[[102]](#cite_note-103) February of the following year, *Film Divider* reported that the twins [Tomax and Xamot](/wiki/Tomax_and_Xamot "Tomax and Xamot") and [Matt Trakker](/wiki/Matt_Trakker "Matt Trakker") from the TV series *[M.A.S.K.](/wiki/M.A.S.K._(TV_series) "M.A.S.K. (TV series)")* will be appearing.[[103]](#cite_note-104) In April, the studio hired Aaron Berg to write the film, and [D. J. Caruso](/wiki/D._J._Caruso "D. J. Caruso") to direct the film.[[104]](#cite_note-105)[[105]](#cite_note-106) November 2015, *Deadline* reported that [Akiva Goldsman](/wiki/Akiva_Goldsman "Akiva Goldsman") will lead a writers room for the next *G.I. Joe* film.[[106]](#cite_note-107) In September 2016, Byung-Hun Lee told LRM Review that the studio doing the third film is waiting on the actors to return, including Johnson.[[107]](#cite_note-108)

In January 2017, Caruso stated that the script for the crossover movie is now being written.[[108]](#cite_note-109) In May 2017, Dwayne Johnson stated that if the opportunity arises he would appear in any future *G.I. Joe* film, and that he hopes to be a part of the franchise expansion as well.[[109]](#cite_note-110) It was announced in September 2017 that the third film is intended to serve more as a reboot of the franchise.[[110]](#cite_note-111) *The Hollywood Reporter* has reported that Paramount announced the film will be released on March 27, 2020.[[111]](#cite_note-112) In May 2018, *That Hashtag Show* reported that the film will be titled *G.I. Joe: Ever Vigilant* with [Josh Appelbaum](/wiki/Josh_Appelbaum "Josh Appelbaum") and [André Nemec](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Nemec "André Nemec") writing the film's script and that the studio are hoping to have Johnson sign on to reprise the role of Roadblock. The plot will reportedly feature his character assembling a new team of Joes with Dania Janack, Dr. Adele Burkhart, [Wild Bill](/wiki/Wild_Bill_(G.I._Joe) "Wild Bill (G.I. Joe)"), [Barbecue](/wiki/Barbecue_(G.I._Joe) "Barbecue (G.I. Joe)"), [General Flagg](/wiki/General_Flagg "General Flagg"), [Doc](/wiki/Doc_(G.I._Joe) "Doc (G.I. Joe)"), and [Keel-Haul](/wiki/Keel-Haul_(G.I._Joe) "Keel-Haul (G.I. Joe)") joining forces to stop Tomax and Xamot Paoli from launching a dark matter [WMD](/wiki/Weapon_of_mass_destruction "Weapon of mass destruction"). The film is said to feature a cameo role for Cobra Commander.[[112]](#cite_note-113) In an interview with Entertainment Weekly, producer Lorenzo di Bonaventura revealed that some scripts for the sequel are currently in development.[[113]](#cite_note-114)

A reboot of the film series, *[Snake Eyes](/wiki/Snake_Eyes_(2021_film) "Snake Eyes (2021 film)")*, was released on July 23, 2021.
